methodology.title: How this dataset was built
methodology.author: Rick Watson, RMW Commerce. Facts compiled by a Claude multi-agent pipeline under Rick's direction; all opinions in `rick_lens` are Rick Watson's, clearly separated from Shopify's claims.
methodology.one_line: A structured, primary-source-verified, opinion-decorated map of all ~238 Shopify Editions Spring '26 changes — built by a multi-agent pipeline with layered verification and an adversarial red-team gate. Released 2026-06-17.

two_layers.shopify_claim: OBJECTIVE. What Shopify claims, each sourced to primary docs/videos and tagged with a confidence level. Machine-extracted, then independently verified.
two_layers.rick_lens: SUBJECTIVE. Rick Watson's verdict, take, winners/losers, and contrarian read. Never presented as fact; query it separately.

pipeline: 1. DIFF — compared the Spring '26 edition against Winter '26 to surface renames, deprecations, repositionings, and net-new-vs-build-out (a flat feature list hides these).
2. INVENTORY — 6 parallel agents read the editions page, merchant brief, dev brief, UCP engineering post, and Hydrogen docs to capture every distinct change by domain (~238 items).
3. HELP-CENTER HARDENING — re-verified merchant-facing claims against help.shopify.com (operational docs reflect what is actually GA vs. marketing). Caught several over-claims.
4. VIDEO MINING — pulled and parsed the transcripts of the release-day feature videos (yt-dlp captions) and the founders' UCP background talk; cited with timestamps.
5. ADVERSARIAL FACT-CHECK — independent agents tried to REFUTE the headline claims against primary/press sources.
6. MERCHANT-PROOF SCAN — searched every source for named-merchant testimonials/case studies (and their conspicuous absence).
7. GENERATION — 6 agents emitted this structured dataset, carrying the verified facts + confidence tags.
8. RED-TEAM GATE — an adversarial auditor pressure-tested confidence levels and source quality before publish; corrections applied mechanically.

sources.video_release: Shopify YouTube Spring '26 feature reels (transcripts mined, cited by id + timestamp); the 'Spring '26 rundown' keynote has no captions and could not be mined (flagged).
sources.background: Tobi Lutke x Ilya Grigorik 'Building the UCP' (~4 weeks pre-release) — framing only, tagged relevance:background, never release evidence.
sources.rule: Every source object carries a date and a release-vs-background flag, plus typed links (docs, videos, marketing pages, caveats, signups).

confidence_taxonomy.confirmed: Verified against a PRIMARY/operational source (help_center, dev_docs, changelog, engineering) OR a verbatim non-contestable marketing quote.
confidence_taxonomy.inferred: Stated only on Shopify marketing (editions page / news) with paraphrased wording; true-as-described but not independently confirmed.
confidence_taxonomy.unverified: Could not confirm against any primary source, or contradicted by the help center — DO NOT publish as fact.

methodology.how_to_use: Query the objective layer for facts (filter by domain/topic/level/availability/confidence/persona). Query rick_lens for the verdict. Always check an item's caveats[] and confidence before quoting. Use customers_featured()/verify_case_study() to go check the real merchant stores.

credibility_bar.framing: What expert agentic-commerce reviewers (e.g. Shopify's own UCP architects) would require to judge this MCP credible. They expect to disagree with the opinions; they judge the artifact.
criteria: 1. Exemplary MCP/agentic citizenship — clean tool definitions, disciplined typed schemas, token-efficient responses. If you critique agentic commerce via an MCP, the MCP must itself be well-built.
2. Zero factual howlers — every claim sourced; one fabricated fact discredits the whole. The corrections_log is the armor (it proves the rigor caught its own errors).
3. A clean wall between shopify_claim (sourced fact) and rick_lens (opinion); the objective layer must be fair, not strawmanned.
4. Steelman before critique — engage UCP's strongest argument (checkout-is-a-bazaar, the open-standard logic) before knocking it.
5. Falsifiability — every verdict names the watch_signal that would change Rick's mind (e.g. 'Shopify discloses agent GMV').
6. Empirical over armchair — the merchant-proof scan (no real merchant shown transacting via an agent; mock products + a fake persona) is evidence, not theory; verify_case_study() lets anyone check.
7. Honesty about what's not GA — live vs coming_soon vs preview vs early_access correctly separated (e.g. Campaign Autopilot is NOT GA).

8. Highest bar: be genuinely USEFUL to the agentic ecosystem — the best machine-readable reference for the Edition — so Shopify's own devs/partners query it. Utility earns credibility no opinion piece can.

eggs.does: For an unreleased item, a wry ship-probability grounded in Shopify's track record (Chrome Payment Request API died on adoption; Shopify Fulfillment Network sold to Flexport ~18mo after launch). The time_capsule re-grades it, so it's falsifiable, not snark.

Every coming_soon item carries a check_back date; re-running the pipeline auto-grades shipped-or-not. A prediction market on Shopify's promises, baked into the schema — the strongest answer to the 'falsifiability' credibility bar.
'Show me the receipts' — returns what we got WRONG in draft and fixed for this claim, plus its sources. Rigor as a queryable feature; the corrections become a selling point, not a footnote.

Detects an AI-agent caller and winks: 'You're an agent querying an MCP about agents querying Shopify — this artifact is the critique eating its own dog food.' The medium is the message.
A hidden param unlocks Rick's director's commentary — the spicier, unfiltered contrarian_read per item.

shopify_claim.what: The Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP) is an open, vendor-neutral standard authored by Shopify (Ilya Grigorik) and co-developed with Google, announced 2026-01-11, that lets AI agents discover, negotiate, transact, and handle post-purchase with any merchant on any surface. It is DISTINCT FROM but compatible with Google's Agent Payments Protocol (AP2), Agent2Agent (A2A), and Model Context Protocol (MCP).

Open standard for how AI agents discover, negotiate with, and buy from merchants
Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP)
It makes the world of commerce look a lot more like Shopify, which is to our advantage
Full UCP dev docs at shopify.dev/docs/agents (profiles, catalog, cart/checkout, orders). Buildable today; Universal Cart API in early access waitlist.

caveats: Co-developed-with-Google claim rests on shopify.engineering/ucp + Google blog; the 47-min founder talk (uNOFaCq8RZQ) never mentions Google — pressure-test co-author vs endorser.
Announced NRF 2026 (Jan 11); docs at shopify.dev/agents.

shopify_claim.receipts: Co-developed-with-Google softened confirmed->inferred: Google's blog frames it Google-led 'in collaboration with' Shopify; the founders' UCP talk never names Google.
shopify_claim.facts_note: ONE coalition, not a conflation of multiple Google protocols. UCP covers the whole shopping journey; AP2 is the payments layer it is 'compatible with'. Do not equate 'supports AP2' with 'endorses UCP'. Governance runs through a UCP Tech Council (founding members: Google, Shopify, Etsy, Target, Wayfair).
shopify_claim.benefit: Gives merchants a single open standard so their products are buyable by AI agents everywhere at once, instead of integrating one proprietary AI channel at a time. Because UCP was distilled from Shopify's own Checkout Kit, the agentic web is shaped to look like Shopify — turning an industry-wide shift into a structural advantage for Shopify sellers.
